serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Sagwala is approximately 250 g, comprising mutton pieces in spinach-based gravy, commonly served alongside rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Sagwala — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Sagwala (cr_id G00010681). All core macros and micronutrients reflect the HPB-reported values at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb/mutton curry reference (USDA SR Legacy item 17174 and composite curry data) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on lamb/mutton curry with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Sagwala. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous meat-and-vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is dominated by protein (14.1 g/100g) and fat (11.6 g/100g) with modest carbohydrate (7.4 g/100g) from spinach and tomatoes, which are low-GI vegetables. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g — borderline-to-high for a single dish component
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments; the potassium content (210 mg/100g) from spinach provides partial offset but is insufficient to neutralise the sodium load at typical serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.4 g/100g), low GI (~35), high protein (14.1 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Sagwala is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content, low estimated GI, and high protein, which together support glycaemic stability; however, it should be paired with low-GI staples such as basmati rice or roti rather than white rice or naan to avoid postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g estimated), cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and sodium (467 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat and cholesterol content from mutton, combined with elevated sodium, present a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with CVD should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request preparation without added ghee or cream, and ensure the spinach component is retained for its potassium and antioxidant benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid / omega-6 pro-inflammatory pathway); tomatoes as nightshade
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton is a significant source of arachidonic acid, which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting mutton with chicken or fish to retain the saag preparation while reducing pro-inflammatory load, and should monitor for joint flares associated with nightshade vegetables such as tomatoes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Sagwala (also spelled Mutton Saagwala or Mutton Saag) is a North Indian and Pakistani curry dish consisting of tender mutton pieces slow-cooked in a richly spiced spinach (saag) puree base, typically incorporating ginger, garlic, tomatoes, onion, and a blend of aromatic spices such as cumin, coriander, garam masala, and turmeric. The dish is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is a staple of the Indian subcontinent's culinary tradition. The texture is thick and hearty, with the spinach puree providing a deep green, slightly earthy sauce that coats the mutton pieces. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced, and umami-rich, with the iron and mineral content of spinach complementing the protein density of mutton. Per 100 g, the dish provides 192 kcal, 14.1 g protein, 11.6 g fat, and 7.4 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium at 467 mg. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low estimated GI (~35), but requires sodium reduction for hypertensive individuals. The red meat content raises concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat, cholesterol, and arachidonic acid. Spinach contributes potassium (210 mg/100g), calcium (50 mg/100g), iron (1.29 mg/100g), and magnesium (17 mg/100g). The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-suitable when prepared without alcohol-based marinades.
common_user_queries: Is mutton saag good for diabetes?
Is mutton sagwala high in protein?
Can I eat mutton sagwala with hypertension?
Mutton saag vs chicken saag nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010681) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb/mutton curry reference and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names present for Indian/Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lamb/mutton curry composite data (closest approved-source equivalent) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; this is documented as an adapted value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this traditional Indian preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on meat-and-vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Sagwala was found in any approved source. Halal suitability is assumed based on standard restaurant preparation; individual preparation may vary.
